transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Sydney. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, thank you for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my stomach issues lately. It’s just been really hard to deal with, and I can’t help but worry about whether things will get better. I feel like I’m constantly trying to manage it, but sometimes it just feels overwhelming.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like your stomach issues have been a significant source of stress for you, and that managing them feels quite overwhelming at times. I can imagine how worrying about whether things will improve adds to that weight. Can you share a bit more about what specific aspects of this situation feel the most challenging for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s definitely challenging. I think what really gets to me is the uncertainty. I keep wondering if I’m doing everything right or if there’s something I’m missing that could help. I find myself avoiding certain foods just in case they might make things worse, but then I worry that I’m being too restrictive. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle of trying to follow advice while still feeling anxious about what might happen next.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the uncertainty is really difficult for you, and it creates a cycle of anxiety around managing your diet and symptoms. You’re trying to follow advice, but at the same time, you’re concerned about being too restrictive. That must feel frustrating. When you think about this cycle, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s really frustrating. I often feel anxious and worried, like I’m constantly on edge. There’s this fear that my stomach problems might not heal, and that makes me tense all the time. Sometimes I feel scared, like I’m not in control of my own body, which is a really unsettling feeling. It’s hard to shake off those thoughts, and they just keep coming back.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of anxiety and fear, particularly around the feeling of being out of control with your body. That sense of being constantly on edge must be exhausting. The recurring thoughts and worries can really take a toll. When you think about those feelings of fear and anxiety, how do they affect your daily life or your ability to do things you enjoy?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It definitely affects my daily life. I find myself ruminating on my health a lot, which makes it hard to focus on anything else. Sometimes I avoid going out with friends or trying new foods because I’m worried about how my stomach will react. It feels like I’m missing out on things I used to enjoy, and that makes me feel even more anxious. I just want to feel normal again, but it’s like this cloud of worry is always hanging over me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds really tough to feel like your health concerns are holding you back from enjoying life and connecting with others. The ruminating thoughts and avoidance seem to create a cycle that adds to your anxiety, making it harder to feel “normal.” It’s understandable to want to break free from that cloud of worry. When you think about the things you used to enjoy, what stands out to you as something you miss the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I really miss being able to go out to eat with friends without worrying about what I can or can’t have. There’s something special about sharing a meal and enjoying the moment, but now I feel like I’m always second-guessing myself. I also miss just being able to relax without that constant nagging worry in the back of my mind. It’s like I’m always on alert, and I wish I could just let go and enjoy things again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like sharing meals with friends was a meaningful part of your life, and it’s hard to feel that joy when worry and second-guessing take over. The desire to relax and enjoy the moment without that nagging worry is completely understandable. It must be frustrating to feel like you’re always on alert. When you think about those moments of connection and enjoyment, what do you think you might need to help you feel more at ease in those situations again?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I need a sense of reassurance that it’s okay to enjoy food again without constantly worrying about the consequences. Maybe if I could find a way to trust my body more, or if I had some strategies to manage my anxiety in those situations, it would help. I guess I also need to remind myself that it’s okay to not have everything figured out and that it’s okay to enjoy life even with these challenges. It just feels like a lot to navigate right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like finding reassurance and trust in your body is really important to you, as well as having strategies to manage your anxiety in those social situations. It’s a lot to navigate, especially when you’re trying to balance enjoying life with the challenges you’re facing. The idea of reminding yourself that it’s okay not to have everything figured out is a valuable insight. How do you feel about exploring some of those strategies or ways to build that trust in your body together?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think exploring some strategies could be really helpful. I’m open to trying new things if it means I can feel more at ease and enjoy those moments again. Building that trust in my body sounds like a good goal, even if it feels a bit daunting right now. I guess I just want to feel like I can manage my anxiety better and not let it control my life so much. I’m willing to give it a shot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s great to hear that you’re open to exploring strategies and working towards building trust in your body. It sounds like you have a clear goal of wanting to manage your anxiety better and regain some control over your life. That willingness to try new things is a positive step forward. 

As we think about strategies, what kinds of approaches have you tried in the past, if any, to help manage your anxiety or build that trust? Or are there specific areas you feel drawn to explore first?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve tried a few things in the past, like deep breathing exercises and trying to focus on mindfulness, but I often find it hard to stick with them when my anxiety spikes. I think I could benefit from more structured approaches, maybe something that feels more practical in the moment. I’m also curious about how to gradually reintroduce certain foods without feeling overwhelmed. It might help to have a plan or some steps to follow. I just want to feel more confident in those situations.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like you’ve already taken some positive steps with deep breathing and mindfulness, even if they’ve been challenging to maintain during moments of high anxiety. Seeking a more structured approach makes sense, especially when you’re looking for practical tools to use in the moment. 

Gradually reintroducing certain foods could be a helpful way to build confidence, too. Having a plan or steps to follow might provide you with a sense of control and clarity. 

Would you like to start by discussing some practical strategies for managing anxiety in the moment, or would you prefer to focus on a plan for reintroducing foods first?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think starting with some practical strategies for managing anxiety in the moment would be really helpful. Having those tools to use when I feel overwhelmed could make a big difference. Once I feel a bit more equipped to handle the anxiety, I’d love to move on to the plan for reintroducing foods. It feels like a good way to build my confidence step by step.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
That sounds like a solid plan, Sydney. Having practical strategies to manage anxiety in the moment can definitely help you feel more equipped to handle those overwhelming feelings. 

One approach that many find helpful is grounding techniques, which can help bring your focus back to the present moment. For example, the 5-4-3-2-1 technique involves identifying:

- 5 things you can see,
- 4 things you can touch,
- 3 things you can hear,
- 2 things you can smell, and
- 1 thing you can taste.

This can help shift your attention away from anxious thoughts and back to your surroundings. 

How does that sound to you? Would you like to explore more grounding techniques or perhaps other strategies that resonate with you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: The 5-4-3-2-1 technique sounds interesting! I like the idea of focusing on my surroundings to help shift my attention away from anxious thoughts. It seems like a practical way to ground myself in the moment. I’d be open to exploring more grounding techniques or other strategies that might help, especially if they can provide some immediate relief when I start to feel overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m glad to hear that the 5-4-3-2-1 technique resonates with you! It can be a really effective way to bring your focus back to the present. 

Another grounding technique you might find helpful is the “breathing box” method. Here’s how it works:

1. **Inhale** for a count of 4 (picture drawing a box in your mind).
2. **Hold** your breath for a count of 4.
3. **Exhale** for a count of 4.
4. **Hold** your breath again for a count of 4.

You can repeat this several times. It combines deep breathing with a visual element, which can help calm your mind and body.

Additionally, some people find it helpful to engage their senses by carrying a small object, like a smooth stone or a piece of fabric, that they can touch when feeling anxious. This can serve as a physical reminder to ground themselves.

How do these techniques feel to you? Do you think they might be useful in your moments of anxiety?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
The breathing box method sounds like a great addition! I like the idea of combining deep breathing with a visual element; it seems like it could really help me focus and calm down. I can see how counting and visualizing a box could create a sense of structure in those moments of anxiety. 

The idea of carrying a small object to touch is also interesting. It seems like it could provide a tangible way to ground myself when I start to feel overwhelmed. I think both techniques could be useful, and I’m definitely willing to give them a try. Thank you for sharing these!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m really glad to hear that you find both the breathing box method and the idea of carrying a grounding object appealing! They both offer practical ways to create a sense of calm and focus when anxiety arises. It’s wonderful that you’re open to trying these techniques; having a toolkit can really empower you in those moments.

As you start to practice these strategies, it might be helpful to notice how they impact your feelings and your ability to manage anxiety. Over time, this can help you build that confidence you’re looking for. 

When you feel ready, we can also explore a plan for gradually reintroducing certain foods. Just let me know when you’d like to shift the focus to that!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Thank you, I appreciate your support! I’ll definitely start practicing the breathing box method and the grounding object. I think keeping track of how they impact my feelings will be really helpful, too. 

When I feel a bit more comfortable with those techniques, I’d love to shift the focus to reintroducing foods. It feels good to have a plan in place for both managing anxiety and gradually working on my relationship with food. I’ll let you know when I’m ready to dive into that!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
You’re very welcome, Sydney! I’m glad to hear you’re feeling positive about starting with those techniques. Keeping track of how they impact your feelings can provide valuable insights and help you see your progress over time.

Whenever you’re ready to shift the focus to reintroducing foods, I’ll be here to support you. It’s great that you’re taking these steps to work on both your anxiety and your relationship with food. Remember, it’s all about progress, not perfection. 

Take your time with the techniques, and feel free to reach out whenever you want to continue our conversation. You’re doing really well!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much for your encouragement! It really helps to know that I have your support as I work through these techniques. I’ll take my time with them and see how it goes. When I’m ready to talk about reintroducing foods, I’ll definitely reach out. I appreciate your understanding and guidance. It feels good to be taking these steps!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
You’re very welcome, Sydney! I’m really glad to hear that you feel supported and encouraged. Taking these steps is important, and it’s great that you’re approaching it at your own pace. 

Whenever you’re ready to discuss reintroducing foods or anything else on your mind, just reach out. I’m here for you. In the meantime, take care of yourself, and I wish you the best as you practice those techniques. You’re doing a wonderful job!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That means a lot to me. I’ll definitely take care of myself and focus on practicing those techniques. I appreciate your support and understanding throughout this process. I’ll reach out when I’m ready to continue our conversation. Take care, and I look forward to talking again soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Sydney! I’m glad to have been able to support you today. Take your time with the techniques, and remember to be gentle with yourself as you navigate this process. I look forward to hearing from you when you’re ready to continue our conversation. Take care, and I wish you all the best in the meantime!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I really appreciate your kindness and support. I’ll keep that in mind as I practice the techniques. I look forward to our next conversation when I’m ready. Take care, and have a great day!
